passages.title: Science and the Stradivarius
passages.source_html:
Science and the Stradivarius: Uncovering the secret of quality


A


Violins made by long-dead Italian craftsmen from the Cremona region are beautiful works of art, coveted by collectors as well as players. Particularly outstanding violins have reputedly changed hands for over a million pounds. In contrast, fine modern instruments can be bought for under £100. Do such figures really reflect such large differences in quality? After more than a hundred years of vigorous debate, this question remains highly contentious, provoking strongly held but divergent views among musicians, violin makers and scientists alike.


B The challenge for scientists


Every violin, whether a Stradivarius or the cheapest factory-made copy, has a distinctive 'voice' of its own. Just as any musician can immediately recognise the difference between Domingo and Pavarotti singing the same operatic aria, so a skilled violinist can distinguish between different qualities in the sound produced by individual Stradivari or Guarneri violins. Individual notes on a single instrument sound different each time they are played, which suggests that the perceived tone of a violin must be related to the overall design of the instrument, rather than the frequencies of particular resonances on it. But although various attempts have been made to analyse such global properties, it is extremely difficult to distinguish between a fine Stradivarius instrument and an indifferent modern copy on the basis of the measured response alone. The ear is a supreme detection device, and a system has yet to be developed which can match the brain's sophisticated ability to assess complex sounds.


C

So how do skilled violinmakers optimise the tone of an instrument during the construction process? They begin by selecting a wood of the highest possible quality for the front and back plates (or parts of the violin), which they test by tapping with a hammer and judging how well it 'rings'. The next important step is to skillfully carve the plates out of the solid wood, taking great care to get the right degree of arching and variations in thickness. Traditional makers optimise the thickness by testing the 'feel' of the plates when they are flexed, and by the sounds produced when they are tapped at different positions with the knuckles.


D


However, in the last 50 years or so a group of violin makers has emerged who have tried to take a more overtly scientific approach to violin making. One common practice they have adopted is to replace the traditional flexing and tapping of plates by controlled measurements. During the carving process, the thinned plates are sprinkled with flakes of glitter and suspended horizontally above a loudspeaker. The glitter forms a pattern each time the loudspeaker excites a resonance. The aim is to interactively 'tune' these first few free plate resonances to specified patterns.


E


Unfortunately, there are very few examples of such measurements for really fine Italian instruments because their owners are naturally reluctant to allow their violins to be taken apart for the sake of science. The few tests that have been performed suggest that the first Italian makers may have tuned the resonant modes of the individual plates – which they could identify as they tapped them – to exact musical intervals. This would be consistent with the prevailing Renaissance view of 'perfection', which was measured in terms of numbers and exact ratios. However, there is no historical data to support this case.


F

Another factor that affects sound quality is the presence of moisture. To achieve the quality of 'vibrancy' in a violin requires high-quality wood with low internal damping. By measuring the pattern of growth-rings in the wood of a Stradivarius, we know that the Italian violin makers sometimes used planks of wood that had only been seasoned for five years. However, such wood is now 300 years old, and the intrinsic internal damping will almost certainly have decreased with time. The age of the wood may therefore automatically contribute to the improved quality of older instruments. This may also explain why the quality of a modern instrument appears to improve in its first few years.


G


Another factor thought to account for sound quality is the nature of the varnish used to protect the instrument. One of the most popular theories for well over a century to account for the Stradivarius secret has been that the varnish had some sort of 'magic' composition. However, historical research has shown that it was very similar to the varnish used today. So apart from the possibility that the Italian varnish was contaminated with the wings of passing insects and debris from the workshop floor, there is no convincing evidence to support the idea of a secret formula.


H


Other researchers, meanwhile, have claimed that Stradivarius's secret was to soak the timber in water, to leach out supposedly harmful chemicals, before it was seasoned. Although this would be consistent with the idea that the masts and oars of recently sunken Venetian war galleys might have been used to make violins, other scientific and historical evidence to support this view is unconvincing.


I


In conclusion, science has not provided any convincing evidence to set Cremonese instruments apart from the finest violins made by skilled craftsmen today. Indeed, some leading soloists do occasionally play on modern instruments. However, the foremost soloists – and, not surprisingly, violin dealers, who have a vested interest in maintaining the Cremonese legend of intrinsic superiority – remain utterly unconvinced.

groups.title: Questions 14-26
groups.source_html:
Questions 14-21


The text has nine paragraphs. Choose the correct heading for each paragraph and move it into the gap. The heading for one paragraph has already been selected.

Options (Word Bank):
A. An analysis of protective coatings
B. Applying technology to violin production
C. Location – a key factor
D. A controversial range of prices
E. Techniques of mass production
F. The advantages of older wood
G. A re-evaluation of documentary evidence
H. The mathematical basis of earlier design
I. Manual woodworking techniques
J. Preferences of top musicians
K. The use of saturated wood
14
正确答案： 14 D. A controversial range of prices  |  15 I. Manual woodworking techniques  |  16 B. Applying technology to violin production  |  17 H. The mathematical basis of earlier design  |  18 F. The advantages of older wood  |  19 A. An analysis of protective coatings  |  20 K. The use of saturated wood  |  21 J. Preferences of top musicians
Question 14

原文：

A段，“Particularly outstanding violins have reputedly changed hands for over a million pounds. In contrast, fine modern instruments can be bought for under £100.”


解析：

原文中提到特别出色的小提琴转手的价格超过100万英而精美的现代乐器不到100英镑就能买到，体现两者价格悬殊。

Question 15

原文：

C段，“So how do skilled violin makers optimise the tone of an instrument during the construction process? They begin by selecting a wood of the highest possible quality for the front and back plates (or parts of the violin), which they test by tapping with a hammer and judging how well it 'rings'. The next important step is to skillfully carve the plates out of the solid wood, taking great care to get the right degree of arching and variations in thickness. Traditional makers optimise the thickness by testing the 'feel' of the plates when they are flexed, and by the sounds produced when they are tapped at different positions with the knuckles.”


解析：

原文描述了小提琴制作者是如何优化乐器音色的具体制作过程。

Question 16

原文：

D段，“However, in the last 50 years or so a group of violin makers has emerged who have tried to take a more overtly scientific approach to violin making.”



解析：

原文中提到一群小提琴制造商试图采取一种更公开的科学方法来制作小提琴，以及后文讲了使用controlled measurements来替代传统做法。



同义替换：

violin making=violin production

scientific approach=technology

Question 17

原文：

E段，“The few tests that have been performed suggest that the first Italian makers may have tuned the resonant modes of the individual plates – which they could identify as they tapped them – to exact musical intervals. This would be consistent with the prevailing Renaissance view of 'perfection', which was measured in terms of numbers and exact ratios.”



解析：

原文中最后提到in terms of numbers and exact ratios，即为数学方法，并且这是最早的意大利制造商使用的方法。



同义替换：

numbers and ratio=mathematical basis

Question 18

原文：

F段，“Another factor that affects sound quality is the presence of moisture.”



解析：

原文中提到水分的存在是影响音质的另一个因素，水分存在于乐器所用的木头内，后文也提到木材的年龄有助于提高旧乐器的音质。

Question 19

原文：

G段，“Another factor thought to account for sound quality is the nature of the varnish used to protect the instrument.”


解析：

原文中提到另一个影响音质的因素是用来保护乐器的清漆的性质，并在后文对此进行了简单的分析。

Question 20

原文：

H段，“Other researchers, meanwhile, have claimed that Stradivarius's secret was to soak the timber in water, to leach out supposedly harmful chemicals, before it was seasoned.”


解析：

原文中提到制作Stradivarius需要将木材浸泡在水中，滤出可能有害的化学物质，然后再进行调试。Xi选项中，生词saturate可能难度较大，意为湿透的。


同义替换：

soak the timber in the water=saturated wood

Question 21

原文：

I段，“Indeed, some leading soloists do occasionally play on modern instruments. However, the foremost soloists – and, not surprisingly, violin dealers, who have a vested interest in maintaining the Cremonese legend of intrinsic superiority – remain utterly unconvinced.”


解析：

原文提到一些著名的独奏家偶尔也会演奏现代乐器，而最重要的独奏家们仍然完全不相信，表明他们更加喜欢Cremonese这类古老乐器。


同义替换：

top musicians=leading soloists，the foremost soloists 

Questions 22 – 26


Choose TRUE if the statement agrees with the information given in the text, choose FALSE if the statement contradicts the information, or choose NOT GIVEN if there is no information on this.

22

The quality of any particular note played on the same violin varies.

正确答案： A. TRUE

定位关键词：

note；the same violin


原文：

B段，“Individual notes on a single instrument sound different each time they are played”


解析：

原文中提到单个乐器上的单个音符每次演奏时发出的声音都是不同的，此题需要了解题目中quality有音色的含义。


同义替换：

single instrument=same violin

different=vary

23

Scientific instruments analyse complex sound more accurately than humans.

正确答案： B. FALSE

定位关键词：

scientific instruments；complex sound


原文：

B段，“The ear is a supreme detection device, and a system has yet to be developed which can match the brain's sophisticated ability to assess complex sounds.”


解析：

题目说科学仪器比人类更准确地分析复杂的声音，而原文指出 耳朵是一种至高无上的探测设备，一种能够与大脑评估复杂

声音的复杂能力相媲美的系统还有待开发。

24

The quality of handmade violins varies according to the musical ability of the craftsman.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

定位关键词：

handmade violins； musical ability ；craftsman.


原文：

C段，“They begin by selecting a wood of the highest possible quality for the front and back plates (or parts of the violin), which they test by tapping with a hammer and judging how well it 'rings'. The next important step is to skillfully carve the plates out of the solid wood, taking great care to get the right degree of arching and variations in thickness. Traditional makers optimise the thickness by testing the 'feel' of the plates when they are flexed, and by the sounds produced when they are tapped at different positions with the knuckles.”


解析：

题目说手工小提琴的质量因工匠的音乐能力而异，而原文只提到工匠的专业的制作技巧，未提到音乐能力。

25

Modern violins seem to improve in their early years.

正确答案： A. TRUE

定位关键词：

modern violins; early years


原文：

F段，“This may also explain why the quality of a moderninstrument appears to improve in its first few years.”


解析：

原文中提到现代乐器在最初几年质量似乎有所提升。


同义替换：

early years=first few years

modern instrument=modern violins

26

Modern violins are gaining in popularity amongst the top violinists.

正确答案： B. FALSE

定位关键词：modern violins；popularity；top violinists


原文：I段，“However, the foremost soloists – and, not surprisingly, violin dealers, who have a vested interest in maintaining the Cremonese legend of intrinsic superiority – remain utterly unconvinced.”


解析：题目中现代小提琴在顶级小提琴家中越来越受欢迎，而原文提到顶级演奏家只是偶尔演奏现代乐器，最重要的独奏家甚至对现代乐器完全不信服。


同义替换：the foremost soloists=top violinists
